title: On the Mathematics of Information-Thermodynamics
abstract: We present a validation of the asdf method, an information-theoretic framework for computing thermodynamic entropy from molecular configurations. The method reformulates entropy estimation as the Shannon entropy of a residual mapping distribution defined between two decorrelated microstates. We demonstrate analytically that for the closed-form Hamiltonians with known solutions, the classical ideal gas and the one-dimensional harmonic oscillator's entropy obtained from the compressibility of the residual mapping object reproduces the exact thermodynamic entropy. In each case, the conditional entropy of the residual mapping object with respect to an uncorrelated microstate is shown to coincide with the ensemble entropy derived from the canonical partition function. These results establish consistency between the asdf formalism and classical statistical mechanics for analytically solvable systems. We further discuss how the framework generalizes to interacting Hamiltonians. The analysis supports the interpretation of thermodynamic entropy as an information measure encoded geometrically in inter-microstate mappings and motivates application of the method to complex condensed phases.
summary: This paper validates the 'asdf' method, a new information-theoretic framework for calculating thermodynamic entropy from molecular configurations. By treating entropy as the Shannon entropy of a residual mapping between microstates, the authors show that the method can recover exact results for ideal gases and harmonic oscillators. The framework is shown to be robust for complex condensed matter systems, including liquids and solids, and can implicitly account for momentum contributions through proper discretization. This provides a powerful, compression-based approach to estimating entropy in large-scale molecular dynamics simulations.
main_problem: The difficulty of computing thermodynamic entropy in interacting and complex systems where closed-form solutions are rare, and the need to validate an information-theoretic framework for entropy estimation.
main_result: The authors analytically and numerically demonstrate that the asdf method accurately reproduces the thermodynamic entropy for the ideal gas and 1D harmonic oscillator, and successfully generalizes to complex condensed phases like liquids and solids.
method: The asdf method uses an information-theoretic approach that reformulates entropy estimation as the Shannon entropy of a residual mapping distribution between two decorrelated microstates.
model_or_system: The study uses benchmarks including the classical monatomic ideal gas and the 1D harmonic oscillator, and extends to complex condensed matter systems such as aluminum, silicon, titanium, and tungsten in both solid and liquid phases.
key_observables: Thermodynamic entropy (S), Shannon entropy (H), entropy of mixing, and thermal de Broglie wavelength.
important_parameters: Particle number (N), temperature (T), thermal de Broglie wavelength (Lambda), and Debye temperature (Theta_D).
main_assumptions: The method assumes the separability of the Hamiltonian and that the momentum contribution can be recovered via a specific discretization scale related to the de Broglie wavelength.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the convergence of the asdf method to the ideal gas analytic solution; Figure 4 illustrates the reduction in variance in the residual mapping compared to raw coordinates; Figure 5 shows marginal coordinate distributions for silicon.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the validation of the asdf method against the ideal gas and 1D harmonic oscillator, moves to the generalization of the framework to interacting systems and mixtures, and concludes with applications to real material systems (solids and liquids) and the integration of electronic/quantum effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Heatmap showing the discrepancy between the asdf method and the analytic solutions for entropy of an ideal gas for 8, 000 ensembles with varying N and Λ. For real systems simulated with sufficiently sized cells, asdf is in good agreement with the analytic solutions.
FIG. 2: The entropy of a microstate Y can be approximated as the conditional entropy of the mapping between an uncorrelated microstate X and Y .

FIG. 3: The information relayed from sender to receiver to construct a microstate Y given a relative microstate X can be constructed as procedural operations over a fixed sequence of the elements in X. For each element in X, information on how the receiver can build elements in Y must be supplied.

FIG. 4: Distribution of vector magnitudes r for raw and mapped (∆) data at varying system sizes for solid aluminum at T = 1000 K. The ∆representation produces a sharply peaked distributions near r = 0 with reduced variance, reflecting a concentration of residual displacements that is favorable for finite-precision encoding.

FIG. 5: Marginal coordinate distributions p(x), p(y), and p(z) for silicon in the diamond phase at 1400 K, shown for both raw and mapped (∆) representations. The transformation from raw to ∆coordinates removes the lattice-scale positional structure and recasts the system in terms of local displacements about a reference configuration. As a result, the periodic features present in the raw coordinate distributions collapse into sharply localized peaks at zero in the ∆representation.
